\appendix
\section{Proof}
\begin{lemma}
  \label{bspnnlemma0}
  Given a system $\mathfrak{S}=(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{X}_0, f)$, $\mathcal{X}_{VF} \subseteq \mathcal{X}$ and any \emph{trajectory} $\tau \in \mathfrak{T}(\mathfrak{S})$, 
  if a $\mathrm{type~1}$ state persistence certificate $B_{sp}$ with respect to $\mathcal{X}_{VF}$ can be found, 
  then it can be guaranteed that $B_{sp}(x_i) \geq 0$, where $i \in \mathbb{N}$ and $\tau = \langle x_0, x_1, \dots \rangle$.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  We proceed by induction on $i$. 
  For the base case, when $i = 0$, we have $B_{sp}(x_0) \geq 0$ according to the property (\ref{bspnn}).
  For the inductive step, we assume that the statement holds for $i = k$, i.e., $B_{sp}(x_k) \geq 0$. 
  When $i = k + 1$, we have $B_{sp}(x_{k+1}) \geq 0$ according to the property (\ref{bsptnn}).
\end{proof}
\begin{lemma}
  \label{state-persistence-lemma-b0-top}
  Given a system $\mathfrak{S}=(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{X}_0, f)$, $\mathcal{X}_{VF} \subseteq \mathcal{X}$ and any \emph{trajectory} $\tau = \langle x_0, x_1, \dots \rangle \in \mathfrak{T}(\mathfrak{S})$, 
  if a $\mathrm{type~1}$ state persistence certificate $B_{sp}$ with respect to $\mathcal{X}_{VF}$ can be found, 
  then it can be guaranteed that $B_{sp}(x_i) \leq B_{sp}(x_0)$ for $i \in \mathbb{N}$.
  % For $\tau = \langle x_0, x_1, \dots \rangle$, if a $\mathrm{type~1}$ trajectory-based persistence certificate $B_{\tau}$ can be found, then it can be guaranteed that $B_{\tau}(x_i) \leq B_{\tau}(x_0)$ for $i \in \mathbb{N}$.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  We proceed by induction on $i$.
  For the base case, when $i = 0$, we have $B_{sp}(x_0) \leq B_{sp}(x_0)$.
  For the inductive step, we assume that the statement holds for $i = k$, i.e., $B_{sp}(x_k) \leq B_{sp}(x_0)$.
  When $i = k+1$, we have $B_{sp}(x_{k+1}) \leq B_{sp}(x_k)$ according to the property (\ref{bspni1}). Combining this with the inductive hypothesis, we have $B_{sp}(x_{k+1}) \leq B_{sp}(x_0)$.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
  \label{state-peristence-lemma-non-increase}
  Given a system $\mathfrak{S}=(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{X}_0, f)$, $\mathcal{X}_{VF} \subseteq \mathcal{X}$ and any \emph{trajectory} $\tau  = \langle x_0, x_1, \dots \rangle \in \mathfrak{T}(\mathfrak{S})$, 
  if a $\mathrm{type~1}$ state persistence certificate $B_{sp}$ can be found, 
  then it can be guaranteed that $B_{sp}(x_i) \geq B_{sp}(x_j)$ for $i \in \mathbb{N}, j \in \mathbb{N}_{>i}$.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  We use double induction. 
  We perform induction on $i$. 
  For the base case, when $i = 0$, we have $B_{sp}(x_0) \geq B_{sp}(x_j)$ according to the Lemma (\ref{state-persistence-lemma-b0-top}).
  For the inductive step, we assume the statement holds for $i = k$, i.e., $B_{sp}(x_k) \geq B_{sp}(x_j)$ for $k < j-1$. 
  When $i = k+1$,  we proceed by induction on $j$.
  For the base case, When $j = k + 2$, we have $B_{sp}(x_{k+1}) \geq B_{sp}(x_{k+2})$ according to the property (\ref{bspni1}).
  For the inductive step, we assume the statement holds for $j=k+1+l$,
  \begin{equation}
  B_{sp}(x_{k+1}) \geq B_{sp}(x_{k+1+l}).
  \label{state-persistence-lemma-b0-top-hp}
  \end{equation} 
  When $j = k + l + 2$, we have $B_{sp}(x_{k+l+1}) \geq B_{sp}(x_{k+l+2})$ according to the property (\ref{bspni1}). 
  We have $B_{sp}(x_{k+1}) \geq B_{sp}(x_{k+l+2})$ according to the inductive hypothesis (\ref{state-persistence-lemma-b0-top-hp}). 
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}
  \label{state-safety-lemma}
  Given a system $\mathfrak{S} = (\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{X}_0, f)$, a trajectory $\tau=\langle x_0, x_1, \dots \rangle \in \mathfrak{T}(\mathfrak{S})$, a labelling function $\mathfrak{L}:\mathcal{X} \rightarrow \Sigma$ and an NBA $\mathcal{A}=(\Sigma, Q, q_0, \delta, Acc)$, if a state safety certificate \(B_{k}(x, q)\) with respect to $q_k \in Q$ can be found, then it can be guaranteed that all runs of all \emph{words} in $\mathfrak{L}(\mathfrak{S})$ can not visit the state $q_k$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
  According to Corollary~(\ref{corollary_tbsc}), any \emph{trajectory} of the system $\mathfrak{S} \times \mathcal{A}$ can not visit $\mathcal{Y}_u$. Therefore, runs of all \emph{words} in $\mathfrak{L}(\mathfrak{S})$ can not visit $q_k$.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}
  \label{transition-persistence-lemma1}
  % Define the acceptable state transitions of the automaton.
  Given a system $\mathfrak{S}=(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{X}_0, f)$, an NBA $\mathcal{A} = (\Sigma, Q, q_0, \delta, Acc)$ and a set of transitions $\Delta_{k, l} \subseteq \delta$, if a transition persistence certificate \(B_{\Delta_{k, l}}\) can be found, then it can be guaranteed that all runs of the words in $\mathfrak{L}(\mathfrak{S})$ make transitions in $\Delta_{k,l}$ happen finitely often.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
We define \(\mathcal{X}_{\Delta}^{k, l}=\{x | (q_k, \mathfrak{L}(x), q_l) \in \Delta_{k,l}\} \). We prove that \(B_{\Delta_{k, l}}\) is a \emph{$\mathrm{type~2}$ state persistence certificate} with respect to \(\mathcal{X}_{\Delta}^{k, l}\). The properties (\ref{bspnn}, \ref{bsptnn}) can be satisfied according to the properties (\ref{bstnn}, \ref{bsttnn}).
We have \(x \in \mathcal{X}_{\Delta}^{k, l}\)  iff  \((q_k, \mathfrak{L}(x), q_l) \in \Delta_{k, l}\) according to the the definition of $\mathcal{X}_{\Delta}^{k, l}$. Therefore, \(I_{\mathcal{X}_{\Delta}^{k, l}}(x) = I_{\Delta_{k,l}}(q_k, \mathfrak{L}(x), q_l)\). we have \(B_{\Delta_{k, l}}(x) \geq B_{\Delta_{k, l}}(x^{\prime}) + I_{\mathcal{X}_{\Delta}^{k, l}}(x)\epsilon\) according to the property (\ref{bstni}).
Therefore, it is guaranteed that any trajectory \(\tau\) visits \(\mathcal{X}_{\Delta}^{k, l}\) finitely often, which ensures that the runs of the \emph{word} \(\mathfrak{L}(\tau)\) make transitions in \(\Delta_{k, l}\) happen finitely often.
\end{proof}
